segments.s471-g57: همیشه (adverb) fronts the clause to mean 'always'. The three nouns دل شرم جفت are listed in juxtaposition (no conjunction) and form the things wished for; جفت تو shows a post-nominal possessive (تو after the noun = 'your companion'). باد at the end is the classical optative particle meaning 'may (it be)'. The whole segment reads 'May your heart, modesty, and companion be always (yours)'.
segments.s471-g58: شبستان شاهان is a genitive construct: 'the bedchamber of the kings' (شاهان post‑modifies شبستان). نهفت is a predicative adjective/verb meaning 'hidden, concealed' and here combines with the clitic/pronoun تو as its subject/object: نهفت تو باد = 'may you be concealed'. باد again is the optative particle. Word order is poetic inversion (noun phrase first, then predicate + pronoun).
segments.s471-g59: بیامد is 3rd person past ('he came'). یکی before a noun gives an indefinite reading 'a certain ...'. فیلسوفی here is a poetic/nominal form referring to a philosopher (the dictionary senses list a verbal/abstract sense, but the context uses it as 'a philosopher'). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as' and گرد is used as the verbal stem 'to go about' (here 'as he went about'). The whole segment: 'A certain philosopher came; as he went about...'
segments.s471-g60: سخنهای (plural with -های) is 'words/speeches' and is modified by the following genitive phrase شاه جهان ('the king of the world'). یاد کردن is a light-verb construction: یاد (remembrance/mention) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'he remembered/mentioned'. The verb agrees with the earlier subject (the philosopher). So: 'he recalled/mentioned the words of the king of the world.'
segments.s471-g33: Imperative clause with the object placed before the verb, typical in Persian. دل خویش را (literally “your heart” with خویش split into خوی + ش: خوی ‘disposition’ + ش the possessive) is the definite object marked by را; پر مدارا is a compound adjective meaning “full of gentleness/tolerance.” کنید is the polite/plural imperative “do/make,” so the whole means “make your heart full of gentleness.”
segments.s471-g34: Another imperative clause with مرا (me) as the object (here split into م + را in the word list). در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase “in the world.” نام دارا کنید literally “make (me) having name,” where دارا (possessor) functions adjectivally to mean “renowned/possessing a name,” and کنید is the imperative “make.” The sense: “make me famed in the world.”
segments.s471-g35: Prepositional/directive phrase: سوی روشنک “toward/to Roshanak.” همچنین “likewise/also” modifies نامه‌ای “a letter” (indefinite — -ای ending). Word order is poetic: direction + addressee, then a note that a letter was likewise sent/issued.
segments.s471-g36: ز introduces the source in poetic register (“from”). شاه جهاندار is a compound title “the king, the world-holder/sovereign.” خودکامه‌ای (خودکامه + -ی) characterizes that king as self-willed/autocratic; the -ی here gives a descriptive/indefinite flavor. Together: “a letter from the autocratic sovereign king.”
segments.s471-g37: نخست “first/at first” sets the temporal order. آفرین here functions as “praise/acclamation” and کرد is the past-tense verb “he did/offered”; بر introduces the indirect object (“to/unto”). So: “First he offered praise to…” (i.e., he began by praising).
segments.s471-g38: A string of epithets in apposition to کردگار: جهاندار و دانا و پروردگار — each term adds a title/attribute (“sovereign, wise, and nurturer/Lord”). The conjunction و simply links the series; the whole sequence describes the Creator being praised.
segments.s471-g39: دگر گفت is a short clause meaning “again/then he said” (دگر here = ‘again/next’). کز is the contracted poetic form of که + از meaning “that/from which” or simply introducing a subordinate clause (“that”). گوهر پادشا is a genitival phrase “the jewel/essence of (a) king,” where گوهر is better read as “essence/gem.”
segments.s471-g40: نزاید is a poetic present/past form meaning “does not arise/is not born” (from زادن). مگر is the exclusory particle “except/unless.” مردم پارسا is the exception phrase “pious people” (مردم = people, پارسا = pious/virtuous). The clause means “is not born except from pious people.”
segments.s471-g1: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’ (polite past). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (‘so that’), and پیش او شد دبیر is a verbless/periphrastic clause where شد (past of شدن) means ‘became/was made / came to be’ — together: ‘he ordered so that the scribe should come before him.’ Word order is subject-verb then subordinate clause: main verb بفرمود + تا + clause.
segments.s471-g2: قلم خواست is a simple past action ‘he wanted/asked for a pen’ (قلم = pen, خواست = wanted/asked). The following noun phrase چینی و رومی حریر lists adjectives and a noun with the noun after two coordinated adjectives: ‘Chinese and Roman silk.’ In Persian the noun (حریر) can follow two adjectives linked by و and modify both.
segments.s471-g3: نویسنده (the scribe) is the agent. از کلک چون خامه کرد is a causal/comparative construction: از کلک ‘from the reed’ + چون خامه (’as/like a quill’) + کرد (did/made) — i.e., ‘he made the reed like a quill.’ کرد is the simple past ‘made’ here, and چون marks the comparison.
segments.s471-g4: سوی + indirect object + noun + کرد is a common dative construction: سوی مادر روشنک نامه کرد = ‘(he) wrote a letter to Mother Roshanak.’ سوی = ‘to/toward,’ نامه = ‘letter,’ and کرد is the past-tense verb ‘did/wrote’ used here for composing the letter.
segments.s471-g5: که here introduces a subjunctive/optative clause — ‘that’ / ‘may it be that’. یزدان is the subject ‘God’. ترا (written as ت + را in the line) is the enclitic 2nd‑person object meaning ‘to you’ (the را is the object/definite marker attached). مزد نیکان is a genitive phrase ‘the reward of the good/righteous’ (مزد head noun followed by نیکان). دهاد is an archaic/poetic present/optative form of ‘دادن’ meaning ‘gives’ or here ‘may give’.
segments.s471-g6: بداندیش ‘the ill‑wisher / malicious one’ is the direct object and is marked by را. درد پیکان is a compound noun phrase ‘pain (درد) of arrows (پیکان)’ = arrow‑wound/arrow‑pain. دهاد is the same verb as before (implied subject still یزدان) meaning ‘gives’ or ‘inflicts’ in the same optative/poetic sense; the subject is omitted and carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s471-g7: نوشتم is the 1st‑person past tense ‘I wrote’. یکی here is the indefinite/indeterminate ‘a certain / one’ modifying نامه‌ای; نامه‌ای is the noun ‘a letter’ with the indefinite suffix -ی. پیش ازین is a temporal phrase ‘before / previously’ (پیش = before, از + ین = ‘of/this’ forming ‘before this’).
segments.s471-g8: نوشته here is a noun ‘the writing / written text’. درو is a contraction of در او ‘in it’ (i.e. ‘in that [writing]’). دردها is the plural ‘pains / afflictions’. بیش ازین is a comparative phrase ‘more than these / greater than this’ (بیش = more, ازین = than this). The clause means that in that written text the speaker recorded/related more pains than the present ones.
segments.s471-g41: دلارای is the name or epithet (vocative/nominal). The following three items are qualities introduced by با "with": با نام = 'with a name/reputation' (i.e. famed), با رای = 'with counsel/opinion' (i.e. wise or of good counsel), و شرم adds 'and modesty' (شرم here = modesty/shame). The list is piled after the name as attributive characteristics; this is a compact, poetic noun phrase.
segments.s471-g42: سخن گفتن is the verbal noun + infinitive pattern 'speaking' or 'to speak'. خوب here modifies the manner of speaking ('well' or eloquently). و introduces a parallel nominal phrase آوای نرم = 'a gentle/soft voice' where آوای is the noun 'voice' (or melody) and نرم is its adjective. The construction describes how she spoke (manner + voice).
segments.s471-g43: پدر is the subject 'father'. مر is an older/poetic form meaning 'to me' (dative). ت (written here as a separate particle) is the 2nd‑person object suffix/element of ترا ('you'), and را is the definite/object marker. پیش ما را = 'into our presence/keeping' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', ما = 'us/we', and the second را again marks the object/emphasis). سپرد is the past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over'. Word order is subject — indirect object pronoun — direct object — prepositional phrase — verb, typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s471-g44: وزان is a fused particle meaning 'and from that' (و + زان). پس شد = 'then he became' or 'after that it happened' (شد is past 'became/happened'). و links to the following clause. نام نیکی ببرد is a compact verbal phrase: نام نیکی = 'a good name' (reputation) and ببرد (here) means 'he took/obtained' — in epic diction this conveys 'he gained/acquired a good reputation.' The verb form ببرد is literary and expresses the action of taking/achieving.
segments.s471-g45: چو means 'when'. آیی is the present/subjunctive form 'you come' (2nd person). The two location nouns شبستان و مشکوی form a coordinated phrase 'night-chamber and musk-scented (bower)'; مشکوی is an adjectival form 'musk-scented/of musk'. من at the end is the possessive 'my' applying to the preceding places (poetic omission of an ezāfe is common), so 'my night-chamber and musk-scented bower.'
segments.s471-g46: ببینی ('you will see' / 'you will find') is the verb introducing the result or discovery. The clause تو باشی is reported/explanatory: 'that you are...' (باشی is subjunctive/present of 'to be' used in subordinate/explanatory sense). جهانجوی من is the predicate 'my world-seeker' (جهانجوی = one who seeks the world; من = my). Together it means 'you will see/find that you are my world-seeker.' The sequence (ببینی + subordinate باشی + predicate) is a compact, poetic way to render 'you will find yourself to be...'.
segments.s471-g47: سر here means 'head' or 'chief' (i.e. foremost). بانوانی means 'of the ladies/women' (an attributive form), so سر بانوانی = 'chief/leader of the women'. و links a second epithet زیبای تاج = 'the beauty of the crown' (زیبای = 'beautiful/beauty', تاج = 'crown'). Poetic compression omits an ezāfe; both phrases act as honorific epithets.
segments.s471-g48: فروزنده is an adjective/noun meaning 'radiant' or 'illuminating'. یاره is 'beloved/companion' (an epithet). و links the next image تخت عاج = 'throne of ivory' (تخت = throne; عاج = ivory). Note the noun + modifier order (تخت عاج) is a common poetic way to say 'ivory throne'. The whole segment gives two more complementary epithets: a radiant beloved and an ivory throne.
segments.s471-g17: نیابد is a negative present/presentive verb (ن- prefix + یابد) in third singular meaning 'does not find'; verb-first order is common in classical/poetic Persian, so the verb precedes its subject کسی ('anyone/no one') here. چاره is the direct object 'remedy/way', and از چنگ مرگ is a prepositional phrase ('from the clutch/claw of Death') modifying what cannot be found.
segments.s471-g18: چو begins a simile/temporal clause ('as/when'); باد is the noun 'wind' and خزانست is a nominal-predicate with the copula -ست ('is autumnal' / 'it is autumn'). The conjunction و links to the independent clause ما همچو برگ where the copula is omitted (elliptical): 'we (are) like a leaf', with همچو ('like') introducing the comparison.
segments.s471-g19: The topical subject جهان یکسر اکنون ('the world, entirely now') precedes the locative/predicative phrase به پیش شماست ('is before you'), where به پیش + pronoun شما forms a prepositional phrase and ست is the copula attached to it. یکسر and اکنون are adverbial modifiers giving scope/time ('entirely, now').
segments.s471-g20: بر اندرز is a prepositional phrase meaning 'regarding the counsel/advice'; دارا فراوان is a nominal phrase ('wealthy ones / many possessors') functioning as the subject/agent, with فراوان as an adjective/adverb 'many / abundant'. گواست is an archaic third-person singular predicate here (used in the sense 'stand as witness / testify' in older Persian) forming the verb of the clause.
segments.s471-g21: که introduces a subordinate/reporting clause. او روشنک را به من داد is a simple past clause: او (he) + روشنک را (Roshanak, definite object marked by را) + به من (to me) + داد (gave). و گفت ('and said') continues the reported speech; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical past 3sg).
segments.s471-g22: Again introduced by که for reported content: چون here means 'how' (introducing the manner of hiding), او is the actor 'he', بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' in present/future sense, and ترا is the second-person object 'you' (here shown as enclitic ت + object marker را). در نهفت is a postpositional phrase with نهفت (past participle 'hidden/concealed') and در ('in/within'), describing where/ how you should be hidden.
segments.s471-g23: کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb fronting the phrase. با introduces accompaniment 'with', followed by two coordinated companions پرستنده ('worshipper/devotee') و دایگان ('nurse/attendant'). This is a simple prepositional/adjunct phrase indicating who accompanies someone.
segments.s471-g24: از ایران is a provenance phrase 'from Iran'; بزرگان پرمایگان is a noun phrase 'great ones / nobles' with پرمایگان as an epithet/qualifier emphasizing rank or multitude — poetic inversion is common, so the two nouns together indicate 'the great nobles (of Iran)'.
segments.s471-g49: نوشتیم is a past tense 1st‑person plural perfect ('we wrote'); in classical/persian poetry this plural can be modesty/authorial. نامه‌بر (compound noun) 'letter‑carrier/messenger' follows the verb by poetic inversion and is the addressee/thing written about; مادرت is 'your mother' with the 2nd‑person possessive suffix -ت attached directly. The sequence is verb + object phrase, a common elevated word order in poetry.
segments.s471-g50: که introduces a subordinate clause (content/purpose: 'that / so that'). ایدر = 'here' marks destination. فرستد is 3rd‑person singular (present/subjunctive/formal) used optatively/subordinately ('should send' / 'may send'). ترا (enclitic object) plus explicit را emphasize 'you' as the object; در + خورت marks the place/destination. Note خورت is a rare/archaic form here and its exact local meaning is uncertain in this passage.
segments.s471-g51: به آیین means 'according to the custom/manner' (به + noun); آیین here takes the sense 'custom/practice' rather than a single ritual. فرزند شاهنشهان is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the son of the shahanshah' (poetic noun‑noun apposition without an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s471-g52: به پیش functions as 'into the presence of / before' (به + پیش). اندرون 'inner quarters' specifies location, and موبد اصفهان is the title + place ('the mobed of Isfahan'); word order stacks locative and office for emphasis typical of epic diction.
segments.s471-g53: This is a coordinate noun phrase listing epithets: پرستنده 'worshipper/devotee', تاج شاهان 'crown of kings' (tāj + plural shahān), و مهد 'and cradle'. Conjunction و links the nouns; no verb is expressed here — a poetic catalogue of titles/attributes.
segments.s471-g54: هم آن را که is a resumptive demonstrative + relative ('also that which...'). خوردی is 2nd‑person singular past ('you ate/drank' / 'you drank'), and ازو marks the source 'from it/from him/her'. شیر و شهد are the items taken from that source ('milk and honey'). Note the object relationships: آن را is the demonstrative object picked up by the relative clause introduced by که.
segments.s471-g55: به مشکوی ما باش is an imperative: باش 'be!' addresses the addressee. به (to/for) + مشکوی ('musk‑scented') + ما ('for us') gives the beneficiary/quality: 'be musk‑scented to/for us.' روشن‌روان is a vocative/epithet 'bright‑souled / radiant spirit' appended after the imperative.
segments.s471-g56: توی در شبستان is a doubled locative ('in / within the bedchamber')—توی and در both mark location, a poetic reinforcement. سر بانوان follows as an attributive phrase: سر here carries the sense of 'top/chief' or 'the midst/headroom of' the ladies, so the whole expresses being present among the women in the inner chamber.
segments.s471-g9: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'. جفت + ت is 'your mate/companion' (جفت = mate; ت = 2nd‑person enclitic). را is the object/definiteness marker attached before the time phrase روز; روز برگشته شد is a verbal phrase formed with برگشته (past participle 'returned') plus شد as an auxiliary, so the whole chunk means 'when the day of your companion's return came.' Word order is inverted/classical: temporal clause introduced by چو, then the possessed noun phrase and the temporal noun with the participle + auxiliary.
segments.s471-g10: به دست is an agent phrase 'by the hand of' (به + دست), followed by یکی 'a certain' modifying بنده‌بر (slave‑carrier). کشته شد is the passive past ('was killed'); شد here is auxiliary forming the passive with کشته (past participle). The whole segment supplies the result/agent: 'he was killed by a certain bendeh‑bar.'
segments.s471-g11: بر آیین شاهان is a prepositional/locative phrase 'according to the custom of kings' (بر = 'according to/on', آیین = 'custom/rite', شاهان = 'of kings'). کفن is the direct object 'shroud' and ساختم is first‑person singular past 'I made'. Word order is topical (manner → object → verb).
segments.s471-g12: ورا is the third‑person object pronoun 'to him/that one'. زین here is a contracted classical form (ز این = 'from this') and introduces the source 'from this world' (جهان). تیز functions adverbially 'swiftly/quickly'. پرداختم is past first‑person of پرداختن; in classical usage here it means 'I dispatched/sent off' (an archaic sense). The clause therefore means 'and I swiftly sent him out of this world.'
segments.s471-g13: بسی here means 'much/many' (intensifier). آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation/peace', and خواستم is first‑person past 'I wished/tried/asked' — together 'I sought much reconciliation.' پیش + جنگ is a temporal phrase 'before the battle.' The verb خواستم governs the preceding object (آشتی).
segments.s471-g14: نکرد is the negative past third‑person of کردن 'did not do/make', taking آشتی as its object: 'he did not make peace.' چون is a causal 'because/since' introducing the reason clause; نبودش places the negation 'there was not' (ش = his/its) and درنگ 'delay/pausing' is the noun complement. The reason reads 'because there was no pause/hesitation (on his part).' Word order is typical classical Persian: main clause then explanatory subordinate clause introduced by چون.
segments.s471-g15: ز خونش is a causal/prepositional phrase 'from/because of his blood' (ز = 'from/because of', خونش = 'his blood'). بپیچید is third‑person past of بپیچیدن 'turned/was turned' (here 'recoiled/turned away'), and هم 'also/even' emphasizes that the enemy (دشمنش) likewise acted thus. Word order places the causal phrase up front and the verb before the subject (classical inversion): 'because of his blood even his enemy turned away.'
segments.s471-g16: به مینو is a prepositional goal 'to heaven' (به = 'to', مینو = 'heaven/paradise'). رساناد is an archaic past‑tense form 'he delivered/brought' (3rd person), with یزدان 'God' as subject and تنش 'his body' as the object (ش = his). The clause literally reads 'God brought his body to heaven.'
segments.s471-g25: فرستید is a 2nd-person plural/polite-form verb form that functions here as an imperative ‘send’ (classical Persian uses past-form shapes for polite imperatives). زودش is زود + ـش (enclitic object pronoun) and means ‘him quickly’ (adverb + clitic object). به نزدیک من is a prepositional phrase ‘to near me / to my vicinity’ with the preposition به followed by نزدیک (adverbial/locational adjective) and the pronoun من as the indirect object ('to me' or 'to my presence').
segments.s471-g26: زداید is a 3rd-person (optative/subjunctive) form of زدودن ‘to remove/cleanse,’ here giving a jussive/optative nuance (‘may he remove’). مگر in older Persian can introduce a subordinate/purpose/optative idea (literary use: roughly ‘so that / perhaps’), connecting the verb to the desired effect; it is not the modern exclusive/exceptive sense here. جان تاریک من is the direct object phrase ‘my dark life/soul’ (جان = soul/life, تاریک = dark, من = my/me).
segments.s471-g27: بدارید is an imperative/plural form of داشتن used here as ‘hold/keep’ or ‘retain.’ The clause چون پیش بود اصفهان uses an inverted, somewhat archaic word order: چون = ‘as,’ پیش بود = ‘was formerly’ (پیش before بود), and اصفهان is the subject placed after the verb; together: ‘keep (it) as Isfahan was before’ / ‘keep Isfahan as it was before.’
segments.s471-g28: ز هر سو is a directional prepositional phrase ‘from every side’ (ز = poetic from, هر = every, سو = side/toward). پراگنده is an adjective/participial describing distribution ‘scattered/widespread.’ کارآگهان is a plural noun ‘the informed/knowledgeable ones’ or officials/agents; here it denotes people dispersed about the region. Word order places the source phrase first (from every side) followed by the predicate/adjective and its noun.
segments.s471-g29: همه کارداران is the noun phrase ‘all the officials/agents’ (همه = all, کارداران = managers/officials). با introduces an accompanying manner or quality ‘with,’ and شرم و داد are coordinated nouns ‘modesty (or shame) and justice’ describing how they acted or were characterized (‘with modesty and justice’).
segments.s471-g30: که is a relative pronoun ‘who/whom/that,’ introducing a clause modifying کارداران. دارای functions as a participial/attributive ‘having/possessing’ or ‘entrusted with’ in this construction. دارابشان is داراب (the personal name Darab) + ـشان (their) and here means ‘their Darab’ or simply ‘Darab (their lord/sovereign)’ in apposition. کار is the object ‘affair/charge,’ and داد is the past-tense verb ‘gave/assigned.’ Together the clause means roughly ‘who were given the charge by Darab’ / ‘to whom Darab entrusted affairs.’
segments.s471-g31: وز here is the archaic fused form (و + ز) meaning ‘and from,’ used as a connective plus a prepositional marker; it is not the noun 'gust.' آنجا = ‘there.’ نخواهید is a future/auxiliary form built on خواستن; in this context it carries the sense ‘you will not be wanting/lacking’ (i.e., ‘you will not lack’). فرمان رواست is a nominal phrase with copula ست: فرمان = ‘command’ (here functioning in the compound sense tied to rule), روا adjective ‘fitting’ (in compounds used to indicate one who carries out commands — hence ‘ruler’), and ست = ‘is.’ The whole segment thus says that ‘from that place you will not be without a ruler’ (literary: ‘you will not lack a sovereign there’).
segments.s471-g32: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase ‘all the cities of Iran’ (همه = all, شهر = city/cities, ایران = Iran). پیش شماست is a locative/linking clause meaning ‘are before you / stand in your presence’ with پیش = before/in front of, شما = you (pl./polite), and ست = is; together: ‘all the cities of Iran are before you.’

sentences.s471-s29: May your heart, modesty, and companion always be yours; may you be concealed in the kings' bedchamber.
sentences.s471-s30: A certain philosopher came; when he went about, he recalled the words of the king of the world.
sentences.s471-s17: Fill your heart with gentleness; make me renowned in the world.
sentences.s471-s18: To Roshanak, likewise, a letter from the autocratic sovereign king.
sentences.s471-s19: First he offered praise to the Creator — the sovereign, the wise, the Lord.
sentences.s471-s20: Again he said that the jewel of a king is not born except from pious people.
sentences.s471-s1: He commanded that the scribe come before him; he asked for a pen and for Chinese and Roman silk.
sentences.s471-s2: The scribe fashioned the reed into a nib and wrote a letter to Mother Roshanak.
sentences.s471-s3: May God give you the reward of the righteous, and may He give the ill‑wisher the pain of arrows.
sentences.s471-s4: I wrote a letter earlier; in that writing I set down pains greater than these.
sentences.s471-s21: Delarā — famed, wise, and modest — spoke well and with a gentle voice.
sentences.s471-s22: My father entrusted you to me (to our care), and from that time he gained a good name.
sentences.s471-s23: When you come into my night-chamber and musk-scented bower, you will see that you are my world-seeker.
sentences.s471-s24: Leader of the ladies and beauty of the crown, a radiant beloved and an ivory throne.
sentences.s471-s9: No one finds a remedy from Death's grasp; the wind is of autumn, and we are like a leaf.
sentences.s471-s10: The whole world is now before you; many wealthy men bear witness to (this) advice.
sentences.s471-s11: That he gave Roshanak to me and said how he should hide you within (safety).
sentences.s471-s12: Now, with the worshipper and the nurse, (I sent him) from Iran (with) many great nobles.
sentences.s471-s25: We wrote to your mother that she should send you here into the court.
sentences.s471-s26: According to the custom of the shahanshah's son, (to) the inner presence of the mobed of Isfahan.
sentences.s471-s27: Worshipper, crown of kings, and cradle — also that from which you drank milk and honey.
sentences.s471-s28: Be musk‑scented for us, O radiant soul, in the women's inner chamber.
sentences.s471-s5: When your mate's day of return came, he was killed by a certain slave‑carrier.
sentences.s471-s6: I made a shroud in the manner of kings, and I swiftly dispatched him from this world.
sentences.s471-s7: I sought reconciliation many times before the battle; he did not make peace, for he would not pause.
sentences.s471-s8: Even his enemy recoiled from his blood; God delivered his body to heaven.
sentences.s471-s13: Send him quickly to near me; may he cleanse my dark soul.
sentences.s471-s14: Hold (keep) Isfahan as it was before; from every side the informed (agents) were scattered.
sentences.s471-s15: All the officials, with modesty and justice, — to whom Darab entrusted the affairs.
sentences.s471-s16: And from there you will not lack a ruler; all the cities of Iran are before you.
